learning_objectives: Analyze why humans are the weakest link in cybersecurity
Apply behavioral psychology principles to security training
Design training programs that change behavior, not just awareness
readings: NIST SP 800-50 Building an Awareness and Training Program

lecture_notes.introduction: Over 80% of breaches involve a human element. Understanding human psychology is essential for building security programs that actually change behavior rather than merely checking a compliance box.
key_concepts: Cognitive biases exploited by social engineers
Behavior change models: COM-B, Fogg Behavior Model
Security culture vs security awareness: knowing vs doing
Motivation: intrinsic (understanding) vs extrinsic (penalties)
Adult learning principles applied to security training
lecture_notes.demonstration: Analyzing real phishing emails with psychological manipulation techniques and designing counter-training

review.value: Behavior = Capability + Opportunity + Motivation. To change security behavior: ensure people have the skills (Capability), the tools and processes (Opportunity), and the desire (Motivation).
Culture is 'how we do things here.' A strong security culture means employees make secure choices by default, not because they are told to. Measured by behavior, not quiz scores.

learning_objectives: Classify social engineering attack types and their psychological triggers
Design training that builds resistance to social engineering
Implement technical controls that support human decision-making
readings: Social Engineering: The Science of Human Hacking Ch. 1-5

lecture_notes.introduction: Social engineering bypasses all technical controls by targeting the human. Understanding the attacker's playbook enables defenders to build resilience at the human layer.
key_concepts: Social engineering types: phishing, vishing, smishing, pretexting, baiting, tailgating
Psychological principles: authority, urgency, social proof, reciprocity, scarcity
Business Email Compromise (BEC) attacks and wire fraud
Defense: verification procedures, reporting culture, technical filters
Physical social engineering: tailgating, badge cloning, dumpster diving
lecture_notes.demonstration: Conducting a simulated vishing call against volunteer students, analyzing the psychological techniques used, and designing defenses

review.value: Attacker impersonates a CEO or vendor, requesting urgent wire transfers. Average loss per BEC incident: $125,000. Defense: out-of-band verification for all financial transactions over a threshold.
The goal is not zero clicks on phishing but high reporting rates. Employees who report suspicious emails enable rapid response. Punishing employees who click discourages reporting.

learning_objectives: Select appropriate delivery methods for different audiences
Create engaging training content that drives retention

lecture_notes.introduction: Effective security training is targeted, engaging, and measurable. One-size-fits-all annual compliance videos have minimal impact on behavior.
key_concepts: Role-based training: executives, developers, general staff, IT admins
Delivery methods: microlearning, gamification, simulations, just-in-time training
Content development: storytelling, real-world examples, interactive scenarios
Training frequency: continuous reinforcement vs annual compliance
Accessibility and language considerations for diverse workforces
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a microlearning campaign on phishing recognition with three short modules, interactive quizzes, and progressive difficulty

review.value: Short (3-5 minute) focused training modules delivered regularly (weekly/biweekly). Higher retention than annual hour-long training. Fits into work schedules without disruption.
Training delivered at the moment of need: a pop-up warning when a user clicks a phishing link, or a reminder when they attempt to send sensitive data via email.

learning_objectives: Design and execute phishing simulation campaigns
Analyze simulation results to target training gaps
Build progressive difficulty campaigns that improve resilience

lecture_notes.introduction: Phishing simulations are the most direct measure of organizational resilience to the most common attack vector. Well-designed programs improve over time.
key_concepts: Campaign design: progressive difficulty (obvious to targeted spear-phishing)
Baseline measurement: initial campaign without prior notification
Targeted follow-up: additional training for employees who click
Repeat offender programs: escalating interventions
Ethical considerations: avoiding public shaming, supporting not punishing
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a three-phase phishing simulation campaign with increasing sophistication, analyzing results, and creating targeted remediation

review.value: Phase 1: Generic phishing (obvious grammar errors, unknown sender). Phase 2: Brand impersonation (Microsoft, shipping companies). Phase 3: Spear-phishing (personalized using real employee and company information).
Never publicly shame employees. Treat clicks as learning opportunities. Provide immediate training (landing page with explanation). Track improvement over time, not individual failure.

learning_objectives: Define metrics that measure behavior change, not just knowledge
Calculate ROI of security awareness investments
readings: How to Measure Anything in Cybersecurity Risk

lecture_notes.introduction: Without measurement, awareness programs cannot demonstrate value or identify where improvement is needed. The best metrics measure what people do, not what they know.
key_concepts: Behavioral metrics: phishing click rate, report rate, time-to-report
Knowledge metrics: quiz scores, training completion (necessary but insufficient)
Culture metrics: employee security survey, observed behaviors, near-miss reporting
Program ROI: incidents prevented, reduced IR costs, insurance premium reduction
Benchmarking against industry averages
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a security awareness metrics dashboard that tracks behavioral change over 12 months, demonstrating ROI to executive stakeholders

review.value: The most important metric: percentage of employees who report suspicious emails. A 70%+ report rate means the organization can detect phishing attempts before they succeed.
Calculate: (phishing incidents avoided x average incident cost) - program cost. If the average phishing incident costs $50K and the program prevents 5 per year, a $100K program has 150% ROI.

learning_objectives: Develop strategies for embedding security into organizational culture
Gain executive sponsorship for security culture initiatives

lecture_notes.introduction: The ultimate goal of security awareness is a culture where security is everyone's responsibility, embedded in daily decision-making, not imposed by the security team.
key_concepts: Security champion programs: volunteer advocates in every department
Executive sponsorship: leadership modeling security behavior
Positive reinforcement: recognizing secure behavior
Security in onboarding: setting expectations from day one
Continuous improvement: feedback loops between incidents and training
lecture_notes.demonstration: Designing a security champion program with recruitment, training, responsibilities, recognition, and success metrics

review.value: Volunteers from non-security departments who receive additional security training and serve as the security team's eyes and ears. They answer colleagues' security questions and promote best practices locally.

Recognize and reward employees who report phishing, follow secure procedures, or identify risks. Public recognition, small rewards, and gamification points. Positive reinforcement is more effective than punishment.

quizzes.topic: Human Factors, Social Engineering, and Training Design

questions.question: A CFO receives an email from what appears to be the CEO requesting an urgent wire transfer of $250,000 to a new vendor. The email was sent from a domain that differs by one letter from the company's domain. Identify the social engineering principles at work and design a three-layer defense (human, process, technical) to prevent this attack.
questions.answer: Psychological principles: Authority (CEO directing a subordinate), Urgency (time pressure reduces critical thinking), Trust (familiar relationship). Three-layer defense: Human: Train executives specifically on BEC attacks with examples of real incidents. Include wire fraud scenarios in phishing simulations targeting finance staff. Process: Implement a dual-approval requirement for all wire transfers above $10,000. Require out-of-band verification (phone call to a known number, not from the email) for new vendor payments or changes to banking details. Technical: Implement DMARC with p=reject to prevent domain spoofing. Deploy email security that flags lookalike domains with visual warnings. Add external email banners indicating the message originated outside the organization.

questions.question: Compare annual compliance-based security training (one-hour video with quiz) to a continuous microlearning program. For each approach, describe: effectiveness at changing behavior, employee engagement, cost, and what metrics you would use to measure success.
questions.answer: Annual compliance training: Behavior change: minimal; knowledge retention drops 90% within 30 days. Engagement: low; employees treat it as a checkbox exercise. Cost: low initial investment but poor ROI per behavior change. Metrics: completion rate (100% due to mandate), quiz score (tests short-term memory, not behavior). Continuous microlearning: Behavior change: significant; regular reinforcement builds lasting habits. 3-5 minute modules weekly keep security top-of-mind. Engagement: higher; interactive, relevant, non-disruptive. Cost: higher ongoing investment but better ROI per behavior change. Metrics: phishing click rate trend (declining monthly), phishing report rate (increasing), time-to-report (decreasing), repeat offender rate (decreasing), security culture survey scores. The microlearning approach is superior for actual risk reduction. Compliance training satisfies regulatory checkboxes but fails to change behavior.

rubric.key: Accurate comparison across all four dimensions

questions.question: After three phishing simulation campaigns, your data shows: Campaign 1 (baseline): 22% click rate, 3% report rate. Campaign 2 (3 months later): 15% click rate, 12% report rate. Campaign 3 (6 months later): 11% click rate, 8% report rate. Analyze the trends and identify a concern in Campaign 3.
questions.answer: Positive trends: Click rate declining steadily (22% to 15% to 11%), indicating improved phishing recognition. Report rate jumped significantly from baseline (3% to 12%), showing employees are actively engaging with the security process. Concern in Campaign 3: The report rate dropped from 12% to 8% while click rate continued to decline. This suggests simulation fatigue: employees recognize the simulations and delete them without reporting. This is problematic because the reporting behavior needs to transfer to real phishing, not just simulations. Recommendations: (1) Vary simulation timing and templates to prevent pattern recognition. (2) Reinforce that reporting is the desired behavior, even if unsure. (3) Introduce rewards for reporting (gamification points, recognition). (4) Separately track reporting of real phishing attempts (not just simulations) to measure true reporting culture.

rubric.key: Identifies positive click rate and report rate trends
Identifies the Campaign 3 report rate concern
Actionable recommendations to address the concern

questions.question: Design a security champion program for a 500-person company with 8 departments. Include: recruitment criteria, training curriculum, ongoing responsibilities, recognition/incentives, and success metrics.
questions.answer: Recruitment: One champion per department (8 total). Criteria: interest in security, respected by peers, good communicator. Volunteer-based with manager approval (10% time allocation). Training: Initial 2-day intensive: threat landscape, common attacks, secure practices, incident reporting, communication skills. Monthly 1-hour updates on current threats and new policies. Annual advanced training on emerging topics. Responsibilities: Monthly team security briefing (15 minutes). First point of contact for colleagues' security questions. Participate in incident response communications for their department. Review and provide feedback on new security policies. Report security concerns and near-misses to the security team. Recognition: Public acknowledgment in company newsletter. Annual 'Security Champion of the Year' award. Professional development budget for security certifications. Direct access to CISO and security leadership. Metrics: Department-level phishing click rates (champion departments vs non-champion), time from policy release to department adoption, number of security questions fielded, near-miss reports submitted, employee security survey scores by department.

rubric.key: Measurable success metrics at department level

major_assignments.title: Security Awareness Program Design
major_assignments.description: Design a comprehensive security awareness and training program for a provided organizational scenario. Include needs assessment, training plan, phishing simulation strategy, metrics framework, and security champion program.
objectives: Conduct a training needs assessment based on organizational risk
Plan a phishing simulation program with progressive difficulty
Build a metrics framework measuring behavior change
deliverables: Role-based training curriculum with content outlines
Metrics dashboard design and ROI projection

criteria: Role-based curricula for at least 4 roles
Engaging training design that drives behavior change
Well-structured simulation program with ethical safeguards

weekly_quizzes.description: Assessment of human factors and awareness program concepts

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on phishing simulation, content creation, and metrics exercises

midterm_examination.description: Assessment covering social engineering, training design, and simulation

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam covering all security awareness and training topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all class sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences may result in a grade reduction.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late without prior arrangement.
course_policies.collaboration: Group discussions and role-playing exercises are collaborative. All written deliverables must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Phishing simulations must only target volunteer participants within the class. Simulating attacks against non-consenting individuals is prohibited.
course_policies.professional_conduct: Social engineering exercises require consent. Respect boundaries during role-playing scenarios.
